paragraphs.text:
Below the sunlit film of the surface, the ocean stacks itself into five
      pelagic zones — each one denser, darker, and more silently violent than the
      last. Pressure climbs by roughly one atmosphere for every ten meters
      descended; at the Challenger Deep, every square centimeter of hull bears
      the weight of a small car.

Pressure scales linearly with depth. The chart below plots ten iconic ocean
    features, from a coral reef shallow to the Mariana's Challenger Deep,
    against their characteristic pressure in atmospheres.
An interactive infographic atlas of ocean depth zones, pressure physics, and the strange biology that survives in the dark. All diagrams rendered as inline SVG.

items.text:
01
        Epipelagic
        0 – 200 m. Sunlight powers nearly all marine photosynthesis here.
        ≤ 20 atm · 12 – 28 °C

02
        Mesopelagic
        200 – 1,000 m. Twilight. Bioluminescence becomes the dominant signal.
        20 – 100 atm · 5 – 12 °C

03
        Bathypelagic
        1,000 – 4,000 m. The midnight zone. Zero sunlight; constant 4 °C.
        100 – 400 atm · ~4 °C

04
        Abyssopelagic
        4,000 – 6,000 m. Vast cold plains; sparse, specialised life.
        400 – 600 atm · 2 – 3 °C

05
        Hadalpelagic
        6,000 – 11,034 m. Trench systems. Extremophiles only.
        600 – 1,100 atm · 1 – 2 °C
